cases.title: Diarrhoea (normal anion gap metabolic acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 70-year-old from a nursing home presents with volume depletion following ongoing lower gastrointestinal fluid loss.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise normal anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: The pH is in the normal range, but the low HCO3 still indicates a primary metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation. Low HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~26.0 (+/-2); measured 26.6 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 140 - (120 + 12) = 8, which is normal, consistent with NAGMA such as diarrhoea.

cases.title: Vomiting (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 35-year-old presents with weakness and dehydration after ongoing gastrointestinal fluid loss.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise metabolic alkalosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Elevated HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic alkalosis. Expected compensatory PaCO2 is ~45.6; measured 44.3 is appropriate, supporting metabolic alkalosis with expected respiratory compensation, as seen with vomiting.

cases.title: Opioid toxicity (acute respiratory acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 27-year-old presents with reduced consciousness, shallow respirations, and pinpoint pupils.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory acidosis due to hypoventilation with appropriate compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH = acidaemia. High PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory acidosis. For acute respiratory acidosis, expected HCO3 is ~25.9; measured 26.8 is appropriate acute compensation. This pattern fits acute hypoventilation, such as opioid toxicity.

cases.title: Vomiting (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 24-year-old presents dehydrated and weak after several days of persistent upper gastrointestinal losses.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise metabolic alkalosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Elevated HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic alkalosis. Expected compensatory PaCO2 is ~49.1; measured 49.8 is appropriate, supporting metabolic alkalosis with expected respiratory compensation, as seen with vomiting.

cases.title: Panic / hyperventilation (acute respiratory alkalosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 26-year-old presents with acute dyspnoea, dizziness, and hand paraesthesiae with a normal cardiorespiratory examination.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory alkalosis with appropriate metabolic compensation
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Low PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory alkalosis. In acute respiratory alkalosis, expected HCO3 is ~22.0; measured 21.6 is appropriate, consistent with acute hyperventilation such as panic.

cases.title: Lactic acidosis (sepsis)
cases.clinical_stem: 60-year-old presents to ED appearing critically unwell, with increasing oxygen requirements, and febrile.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.explanation: Sepsis commonly causes high anion gap metabolic acidosis due to lactate accumulation.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise metabolic alkalosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Elevated HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic alkalosis. Expected compensatory PaCO2 is ~51.2; measured 52.6 is appropriate, supporting metabolic alkalosis with expected respiratory compensation, as seen with vomiting.

cases.title: Sepsis (respiratory alkalosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 55-year-old presents to ED with hypotensive and with suspected sepsis and with respiratory distress and with rigors.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.explanation: Sepsis commonly causes respiratory alkalosis due to hyperventilation.

cases.title: Opioid toxicity (acute respiratory acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 32-year-old is brought in drowsy with bradypnoea and decreased responsiveness.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory acidosis due to hypoventilation with appropriate compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH = acidaemia. High PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory acidosis. For acute respiratory acidosis, expected HCO3 is ~27.2; measured 28.1 is appropriate acute compensation. This pattern fits acute hypoventilation, such as opioid toxicity.

cases.title: COPD (chronic respiratory acidosis with metabolic compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 65-year-old with chronic respiratory symptoms presents with worsening shortness of breath over several days.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chronic respiratory acidosis with appropriate renal compensation
cases.explanation: pH is low or near-normal with elevated PaCO2, indicating respiratory acidosis. In chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by ~4 mmol/L per 10 mmHg PaCO2 above 40. Expected HCO3 is ~31.2; measured 30.5 is appropriately elevated, consistent with chronic respiratory compensation seen in COPD.

cases.title: COPD (chronic respiratory acidosis with metabolic compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 72-year-old with longstanding breathlessness presents with increased sputum and fatigue.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chronic respiratory acidosis with appropriate renal compensation
cases.explanation: pH is low or near-normal with elevated PaCO2, indicating respiratory acidosis. In chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by ~4 mmol/L per 10 mmHg PaCO2 above 40. Expected HCO3 is ~36.8; measured 36.7 is appropriately elevated, consistent with chronic respiratory compensation seen in COPD.

cases.title: Diarrhoea (normal anion gap metabolic acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 34-year-old presents dehydrated and weak after several days of high-output gastrointestinal losses.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise normal anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: The pH is in the normal range, but the low HCO3 still indicates a primary metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation. Low HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~32.0 (+/-2); measured 32.5 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 139 - (114 + 16) = 9, which is normal, consistent with NAGMA such as diarrhoea.

cases.title: Salicylate toxicity (mixed respiratory alkalosis + HAGMA)
cases.clinical_stem: 21-year-old presents with tinnitus, vomiting, fever, and rapid breathing after an overdose.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

expected_compensation.note: Low PaCO2 and low HCO3 are due to two primary processes: respiratory alkalosis and HAGMA

cases.learning_objective: Recognise the mixed respiratory alkalosis and high anion gap metabolic acidosis of salicylate toxicity
cases.explanation: Both PaCO2 and HCO3 are low. This is not explained by a single primary disorder alone. The low PaCO2 indicates a respiratory alkalosis, while the low HCO3 with raised anion gap (137 - (101 + 14) = 22) indicates a high anion gap metabolic acidosis. This is a mixed respiratory alkalosis and metabolic acidosis, classic for salicylate toxicity.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory alkalosis with appropriate metabolic compensation
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Low PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory alkalosis. In acute respiratory alkalosis, expected HCO3 is ~20.8; measured 21.2 is appropriate, consistent with acute hyperventilation such as panic.

cases.explanation: Low pH indicates acidaemia. High PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory acidosis. For acute respiratory acidosis, expected HCO3 is ~25.2; measured 24.9 is appropriate acute compensation. The anion gap is normal at 10.0. This pattern is consistent with acute hypoventilation.

cases.title: COPD exacerbation (acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis)

cases.explanation: Low pH indicates acidaemia. High PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory acidosis. For acute respiratory acidosis, expected HCO3 is ~26.1; measured 27.0 is appropriate acute compensation. The anion gap is normal at 10.0. This pattern is consistent with acute hypoventilation.

cases.title: Diuretic use (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 68-year-old taking loop diuretics presents with postural symptoms, dry mucous membranes, and fatigue after recent volume depletion.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from diuretic-associated volume depletion
cases.explanation: The pH is near normal, but the elevated HCO3 still indicates a primary metabolic alkalosis with compensation. Elevated HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic alkalosis. Expected compensatory PaCO2 is ~44.2; measured 45.0 is appropriate. Anion gap is 136 - (96 + 30) = 10, which is normal. This pattern is most consistent with diuretic-associated contraction alkalosis from diuretic use.

cases.explanation: Low pH = acidaemia. High PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory acidosis. For acute respiratory acidosis, expected HCO3 is ~27.0; measured 27.4 is appropriate acute compensation. This pattern fits acute hypoventilation, such as opioid toxicity.

cases.explanation: Sepsis commonly causes high anion gap metabolic acidosis due to lactate accumulation.

cases.title: DKA (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 19-year-old presents to ED with recent weight loss, nausea and abdominal discomfort, and deep rapid breathing.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH = acidaemia. Low HCO3 indicates metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~29.0 (+/-2); measured 29.4 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 139 - (105 + 14) = 20 (raised), consistent with HAGMA such as DKA.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory alkalosis with appropriate metabolic compensation
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Low PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory alkalosis. In acute respiratory alkalosis, expected HCO3 is ~21.8; measured 21.8 is appropriate, consistent with acute hyperventilation such as panic.

cases.title: Renal failure (uraemic HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis from uraemia with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH indicates acidaemia. Low HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~29.0 (+/-2); measured 28.3 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 134 - (96 + 14) = 24, which is raised. This pattern is most consistent with renal failure causing uraemic high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

cases.title: Vomiting (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 35-year-old presents with weakness and dehydration after ongoing gastrointestinal fluid loss.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise metabolic alkalosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Elevated HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic alkalosis. Expected compensatory PaCO2 is ~47.7; measured 46.8 is appropriate, supporting metabolic alkalosis with expected respiratory compensation, as seen with vomiting.

cases.title: COPD exacerbation (acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 76-year-old is brought to ED with increasing somnolence and a history of COPD and worsening dyspnoea and wheeze.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.explanation: COPD exacerbations cause acute rises in CO2 on a background of chronic respiratory acidosis.

cases.title: COPD (chronic respiratory acidosis with metabolic compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 65-year-old with chronic respiratory symptoms presents with worsening shortness of breath over several days.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chronic respiratory acidosis with appropriate renal compensation
cases.explanation: pH is low or near-normal with elevated PaCO2, indicating respiratory acidosis. In chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by ~4 mmol/L per 10 mmHg PaCO2 above 40. Expected HCO3 is ~36.4; measured 36.3 is appropriately elevated, consistent with chronic respiratory compensation seen in COPD.

cases.title: Salicylate toxicity (mixed respiratory alkalosis + HAGMA)
cases.clinical_stem: 34-year-old presents confused, tachypnoeic, and febrile with ringing in the ears.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

expected_compensation.note: Low PaCO2 and low HCO3 are due to two primary processes: respiratory alkalosis and HAGMA

cases.learning_objective: Recognise the mixed respiratory alkalosis and high anion gap metabolic acidosis of salicylate toxicity
cases.explanation: Both PaCO2 and HCO3 are low. This is not explained by a single primary disorder alone. The low PaCO2 indicates a respiratory alkalosis, while the low HCO3 with raised anion gap (142 - (104 + 11) = 27) indicates a high anion gap metabolic acidosis. This is a mixed respiratory alkalosis and metabolic acidosis, classic for salicylate toxicity.

cases.title: Diarrhoea (normal anion gap metabolic acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 29-year-old presents with light-headedness and dehydration after several days of gastroenteritis symptoms.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise normal anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: The pH is in the normal range, but the low HCO3 still indicates a primary metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation. Low HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~38.0 (+/-2); measured 37.0 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 139 - (107 + 20) = 12, which is normal, consistent with NAGMA such as diarrhoea.
